LAW AND MOTION, 302, DEFENDANTS MARK WILLIAMS, MARK WILLIAMS, CPA, INC. AND RUZZO SCHOLL & MURPHY'S MOTION FOR GOOD FAITH SETTLEMENT DETERMINATION IS GRANTED. DEFENDANT TODD DUCKSON WHO OBJECTS TO THE GOOD FAITH DETERMINATION BEARS THE INITIAL BURDEN OF CONTESTING THAT THE SETTLEMENT WAS IN GOOD FAITH AND THE ULTIMATE BURDEN OF SHOWING THAT THE SETTLEMENT WAS NOT IN GOOD FAITH. DUCKSON CANNOT MEET EITHER BURDEN. THE ONLY REASON HE GIVES FOR CONTESTING THE GOOD FAITH OF THE SETTLEMENT IS THAT HE REMAINS AS A PARTY AFTER THE SETTLEMENT EVEN THOUGH, IN HIS VIEW, HE IS NOT LIABLE TO THE PLAINTIFFS. THAT IS NOT A LEGALLY SUFFICIENT REASON TO CONTEST WHETHER A SETTLEMENT IS IN GOOD FAITH. A SETTLING DEFENDANT HAS NO OBLIGATION TO OBTAIN THE DISMISSAL OF ANOTHER DEFENDANT IN ORDER FOR A SETTLEMENT TO BE DETERMINED TO BE IN GOOD FAITH. THE PREVAILING PARTY IS TO PROVIDE A FORM OF ORDER AND COMPLY WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. REPORTER: MELANIE GHENO, CSR # 7489. (302/HK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination
Off Calendar Dec-14-2015 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS MARK WILLIAMS, MARK WILLIAMS, CPA, INC. and RUZZO SCHOLL & MURPHY'S Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination is granted. Defendant Todd Duckson who objects to the good faith determination bears the initial burden of contesting that the settlement was in good faith and the ultimate burden of showing that the settlement was not in good faith. Duckson cannot meet either burden. The only reason he gives for contesting the good faith of the settlement is that he remains as a party after the settlement even though, in his view, he is not liable to the plaintiffs. That is not a legally sufficient reason to contest whether a settlement is in good faith. A settling defendant has no obligation to obtain the dismissal of another defendant in order for a settlement to be determined to be in good faith. The prevailing party is to provide a form of order and comply with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Jan-16-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. LAWRENCE G. PAPALE, Tel No. (707) 963-1704 appearing for Plaintiffs;
Atty. DANIEL J. MASH of Mash & Bahrick, Tel No. (408) 298-3285 appearing for Defendants Mark Williams, Ruzzo Scholl & Murphy, and Saratoga Family Counsel, Inc.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court modified its tentative ruling, and ruled as follows: DEFENDANTS MARK WILLIAMS, RUZZO SCHOLL & MURPHY, SARATOGA FAMILY COUNSELING, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is granted in part and denied in part. Granted as to the tenth cause of action for unjust enrichment as to Plaintiffs Clive Dorman and Olivia Dorman only. Defendants met their initial burden by showing that they did not receive compensation from the Dormans. (Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF"), Issue 5, nos. 1-6.) Plaintiffs Clive and Olivia Dorman do not submit evidence identifying a direct benefit that Defendants received at the expense of Plaintiffs. Granted as to the fourth cause of action for negligent misreprentation by Plaintiffs Clive and Olivia Dorman against Defendants Williams and MWCPA, Inc. Denied as to the other causes of action. There is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Mark Williams owed the Dormans a fiduciary duty. Plaintiffs present evidence showing that in March 2009 Mark Williams met with the Dormans and instructed them to make an additional investment. (Plaintiffs' Additional Material Facts ("AMF") nos. 12-31.) Thus, even if Williams was not officially hired by the Dormans as their financial advisor, a reasonable finder of fact could find that Williams owed them a fiduciary duty because he voluntarily provided them financial advice and because of his longstanding, close relationship with the family. As to the sixth cause of action for negligent misrepresentation, Defendants did not meet their initial burden. Showing that the Dormans did not officially hire or compensate Williams does not entirely negate the duty element. As to the fifth cause of action for fraud, Defendants met their initial burden by showing that Plaintiffs cannot establish justifiable reliance. (UMF, Issue 2, nos. 7-14.) In opposition, Plaintiffs raise a triable issue of material fact as to justifiable reliance by providing evidence that Plaintiffs trusted Williams' word over the printed materials, given his longstanding relationship with Enid Shoor. (AMF 12.) As to the eighth cause of action, as discussed above, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Williams owed Plaintiffs a fiduciary duty. As to the eleventh cause of action, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Williams was selling securities at the March 2009 meeting. Defendants' objections are overruled, as Defendants did not comply with CRC 3.1354(c). PREVAILING PARTY SHALL PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER. Judge: A. James Robertson, II; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/AJR)
